title: The Winter Fuel Payment explained
description: How the Winter Fuel Payment works for winter 2026 to 2027: who qualifies, the £200 and £300 rates, the £35,000 income rule, and when the money arrives.
blurb: Who gets the Winter Fuel Payment, how much it pays, and how the £35,000 income clawback actually works.
lede: The Winter Fuel Payment has been through more upheaval in the last three years than in the previous twenty. It was universal for pensioners, then restricted to Pension Credit claimants for one winter, and is now back for almost everyone over State Pension age, with a tax clawback for higher incomes bolted on. The result is a scheme that most people half-remember from an older set of rules. This guide sets out how it works for winter 2026 to 2027: who qualifies, exactly how much each household gets, how the £35,000 income rule operates, and what the payment realistically covers once it lands.
short_answer: The Winter Fuel Payment is an annual tax-free payment of £100 to £300 towards heating costs for people who have reached State Pension age. For winter 2026 to 2027 you qualify if you were born before 28 June 1960, and most people are paid automatically in November or December. If your own taxable income is over £35,000, HMRC takes the payment back through the tax system, so in practice it stays with low and middle income pensioners.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for the Winter Fuel Payment
sections.html:
The Winter Fuel Payment goes to people who have reached State Pension age by the qualifying week in September. For winter 2026 to 2027 that means anyone born before 28 June 1960 who lives in England, Wales or Northern Ireland. The date moves each year as State Pension age rises, so always check the current cut-off on the GOV.UK Winter Fuel Payment page before assuming you are in or out.

You do not need to be on benefits, you do not need to own your home, and you do not need to heat with any particular fuel. Despite the name, the money is not tied to fuel at all. It arrives as a normal bank payment and you can spend it on anything.

Scotland runs its own version, the Pension Age Winter Heating Payment, paid by Social Security Scotland. The rules and amounts track the rest of the UK closely, with rates a little higher for 2026 to 2027 after uprating, and the same income clawback applies.

A few situations change what you get rather than whether you get it:

  • If you live with another person who also qualifies, the household amount is usually split between you.
  • If you live in a care home you get a reduced amount, and nothing at all if you are also on Pension Credit, Universal Credit or income-related ESA for the whole qualifying period.
  • If you and a partner jointly claim Pension Credit, one of you receives the full household amount.

sections.h2: How much you get for winter 2026 to 2027
sections.html:
The headline figures are £200 per household where everyone is under 80, and £300 where at least one person is 80 or over. The 80th-birthday test for this winter is being born before 28 September 1946. How that household total is divided depends on who you live with and whether you claim certain benefits.

Your situationBorn 28 Sept 1946 to 27 June 1960Born before 28 Sept 1946
Live alone, or no one else qualifies£200£300
Live with someone else who qualifies (no qualifying benefits)£100 each£150 each if both over 80; £200 if only you are
Get Pension Credit or similar (single claim)£200£300
Joint Pension Credit claim with a partner£200 between you£300 between you
Live in a care home (not on Pension Credit)£100£150

Winter Fuel Payment rates for winter 2026 to 2027, GOV.UK, checked June 2026.

The payment is tax free in itself, and it does not count as income for any other benefit. The only way you hand it back is through the £35,000 rule below.

sections.h2: The £35,000 income rule and how the clawback works
sections.html:
Since winter 2025 to 2026, everyone over State Pension age is paid first, then HMRC takes the money back from anyone whose individual taxable income is over £35,000. Three details matter here.

First, it is your own income, not your household's. A couple where one partner earns £40,000 and the other £15,000 keeps the lower earner's share. Second, the test is taxable income: State Pension, private and workplace pensions, earnings, rental profit and taxable savings interest all count, while ISA interest does not. Third, it is a cliff edge rather than a taper. At £35,001 the whole payment is reclaimed, not a fraction of it.

The clawback itself is mostly invisible. If you pay tax through PAYE on a pension, HMRC adjusts your tax code during the following tax year and the money comes back in slightly higher monthly deductions. If you fill in a Self Assessment return, it is added to your bill there instead. You do not need to write to anyone or send a cheque.

If you know your income will stay over £35,000 and you would rather skip the merry-go-round, you can opt out of receiving the payment altogether through GOV.UK. Opting out is the tidier option for anyone doing Self Assessment who does not want an extra line to reconcile, but it is purely about admin. You end up in the same financial position either way. Check the current opt-out deadline on GOV.UK, as it falls well before the payments go out.

sections.h2: When and how the money arrives
sections.html:
Almost everyone who qualifies is paid automatically. If you receive the State Pension or most other DWP benefits, the payment lands in the same bank account, usually in November or December. A letter arrives in October or November telling you how much to expect, and the bank statement entry includes the letters WFP alongside your National Insurance reference.

A small group does need to claim: typically people who have deferred their State Pension and receive no other qualifying benefit, and some who have recently moved. Claims open in the autumn by phone or post and stay open until the end of the following March, so a missed payment is recoverable for several months.

If the letter says you should be paid and nothing has arrived by mid January, ring the Winter Fuel Payment Centre rather than waiting. Wrong or closed bank accounts are the usual culprit, and the fix is quick once flagged.

One thing the payment no longer depends on is Pension Credit. That link lasted a single winter in 2024 to 2025 and is gone. Pension Credit is still worth claiming if your income is low, because it opens the door to the Warm Home Discount, Cold Weather Payments during freezing spells, housing support and a free TV licence over 75. The Winter Fuel Payment, though, now arrives regardless.

It helps to translate the payment into energy. At current capped rates, gas is far cheaper per unit than electricity, so what the money buys depends heavily on how you heat.

Spent on£200 buys£300 buys
Gas units at 7.33p per kWhabout 2,730 kWhabout 4,090 kWh
Electricity units at 26.11p per kWhabout 766 kWhabout 1,149 kWh
Combined standing charges at 86.23p per dayabout 232 daysabout 348 days

At 7.33p per kWh for gas, 26.11p per kWh for electricity, and combined daily standing charges of 86.23p (57.19p electricity plus 29.04p gas), Ofgem cap July to September 2026.

For a gas-heated home, £200 covers roughly a quarter of a typical year's gas units, which is a meaningful slice of the winter quarter. For a home heated entirely by electricity the same £200 disappears into about three to four weeks of winter heating, which is one reason electrically heated households also lean on the Warm Home Discount.

It is also a reminder that standing charges eat a fixed £315 or so a year before you use a single unit, payment or no payment. And if you want the money to keep paying out beyond one winter, putting £300 towards topping up loft insulation can cut a couple of hundred pounds off heating bills every year thereafter, rather than once.

The Winter Fuel Payment is now close to universal again for people over State Pension age: £200 per household, £300 where someone is 80 or over, paid automatically before Christmas, and quietly reclaimed through tax from anyone with individual income above £35,000. You do not need to apply unless you receive no State Pension or qualifying benefit, and it never affects your other entitlements.

Because the scheme has changed in each of the last three winters, treat any specific figure here as a snapshot. Check the qualifying dates, amounts and opt-out deadline on the official GOV.UK Winter Fuel Payment page each autumn, since the birth-date cut-off moves with State Pension age and governments keep adjusting the design. And once the payment lands, it stretches furthest when you know where your money goes: reading your energy bill properly tells you whether units, standing charges or debt repayments are doing the damage, and that decides whether the best use of £200 is the gas account, a draught-proofing weekend or the loft.

faq.q: Is the Winter Fuel Payment taxable or does it affect my benefits?
faq.a: No on both counts. The payment is tax free and does not count as income for Pension Credit, Housing Benefit or any other means-tested support. The only deduction is the separate clawback: if your individual taxable income is over £35,000, HMRC reclaims the full payment through your tax code or Self Assessment.

faq.q: Do I need to apply for the Winter Fuel Payment?
faq.a: Usually not. If you receive the State Pension or most DWP benefits you are paid automatically in November or December, with a letter beforehand confirming the amount. You only need to claim if you get no qualifying benefit at all, for example because you have deferred your State Pension. Claims stay open until the end of the following March.

faq.q: My income is just over £35,000. Do I lose part of the payment?
faq.a: You lose all of it. The £35,000 rule is a cliff edge, not a taper, so income of £35,001 means the whole payment is reclaimed while £34,999 means you keep every pound. The test uses your own taxable income only, never your partner's, and ISA interest does not count towards it.

faq.q: I live in Scotland. Do I still get the Winter Fuel Payment?
faq.a: Not under that name. Scotland pays the Pension Age Winter Heating Payment through Social Security Scotland instead. The eligibility rules mirror the rest of the UK, the amounts are slightly higher after Scottish uprating for 2026 to 2027, and the same £35,000 income clawback applies. It is paid automatically in the same late-autumn window.

faq.q: Is the Winter Fuel Payment the same as the Cold Weather Payment?
faq.a: No. The Winter Fuel Payment is a single annual payment to pensioner households regardless of the weather. Cold Weather Payments are £25 top-ups paid to people on certain means-tested benefits only when local temperatures average freezing or below for seven consecutive days. You can receive both in the same winter if you qualify for each.
